1:
The whole cloud of witnesses reads this verse the same way: when Herod Antipas hears the fame of Jesus, a guilty conscience makes him conclude that the man he beheaded has risen. The differences are of approach, not of substance.

The grammatical-historical commentators do the spadework. Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott and Robertson identify Antipas as a son of Herod the Great who ruled Galilee and Perea, the title tetrarch meaning ruler of a fourth part, though loosely used for any petty king (compare v.9). They register surprise that he had not heard of Jesus before — Robertson calls it ‘rather surprising’ — and Ellicott suggests his informants may have been courtiers such as Manaen (Acts 13:1), Chuza (Luke 8:3), or the nobleman of John 4:46. They note the parallels in Mark 6:14 and Luke 9:7.

The Greek and Latin fathers press the moral and harmonising questions. Chrysostom (Antiochene) observes that the powerful learn such things slowly because they care little for them, yet marvels that virtue is so great that Herod fears John even after death. Augustine, with the Catena, labours to reconcile Matthew’s ‘at that time’ with the different order in Mark and Luke. Jerome rejects any notion of metempsychosis, and Remigius distinguishes this Herod from the Herod of the nativity (Matthew 2:16). Raban draws the contrast that the Gentile heart believed in resurrection more readily than the Jewish, while Origen (Alexandrian) catalogues the Sadducean and Pharisaic opinions on whether the dead rise.

The Reformed voices read providentially: Calvin holds that the report spreading even into Herod’s court left the Jews without the excuse of ignorance, and Gill fixes the identity of the tetrarch. The Puritan Matthew Henry presses the conscience — ‘blood cries from the heart of him that shed it’ — and warns that there may be the terror of strong convictions where there is not the truth of saving conversion (compare Proverbs 28:1). Wesley and Clarke add only that this was about a year into the public ministry. Origen further probes the popular psychology, questioning how one so widely known as Jesus could be mistaken for a man newly beheaded, and the witnesses agree that it was dread, not sober reasoning, that drove Herod to his verdict; the rumour was the natural offspring of a court haunted by a fresh and bloody guilt.
2:
On Herod’s words to his servants — ‘This is John the Baptist; he is risen from the dead’ — the witnesses agree that a stricken conscience, not theology, speaks. The discussion turns on a single historical puzzle: how a man inclined to Sadducee opinions could confess a resurrection.

The grammatical-historical expositors supply the framework. Lightfoot asks whether Herod was a Sadducee, comparing the ‘leaven of the Sadducees’ in Matthew 16:6 with the ‘leaven of Herod’ in Mark 8:15, and concludes that the confession was wrung from a conscience pricked with horror. Ellicott makes the same identification with the Sadducean priestly party and finds it remarkable that superstitious terror overcame his scepticism — to Herod the new Prophet was ‘nothing less than John himself.’ Barnes notes that others guessed Elijah or one of the prophets (Mark 6:15). Robertson glosses the courtiers (‘boys’) and the verb ‘work in him’ (energousin), adding Bruce’s quip that ‘the King has the Baptist on the brain.’

The Reformed Calvin gives the fullest reading: the conjecture arose first among the people, out of hatred for the tyrant and detestation of the murder, and Herod, as bad consciences do, readily believed what he dreaded. Calvin notes the popular misuse of Malachi’s promise of Elijah (Malachi 4:5), wrongly applied to the literal return of 1 Kings 17:1, when the true Redeemer was already at hand. Gill simply reports Herod’s fearful familiarity with his household.

The Wesleyan voices sharpen the irony. Clarke notes the Codex Bezae addition ‘whom I beheaded,’ exclaiming ‘see the power of conscience!’ — a guilty conscience needs no other accuser, yet offers no salvation from sin. Wesley puts it epigrammatically: Herod was a Sadducee, ‘but Sadduceeism staggers when conscience awakes.’ The shared verdict is that Herod’s creed bent before his guilt; the miracles attributed to a risen John (who in life did none, John 10:41) were the projection of his fear. Robertson recalls Josephus’s tale that the ghosts of Alexander and Aristobulus haunted the palace of Herod the Great, and Ellicott detects, mingled with the terror, ‘the wider unbelief of Roman epicureanism’; yet across the voices the verdict is one — the superstitious dread of a guilty conscience proved stronger than any sceptical creed.
3:
The verse turns back to recount why John was imprisoned — ‘for Herodias’ sake.’ The witnesses are united that the cause was John’s reproof of an unlawful marriage, and they fill in, with one voice, the tangled genealogy behind it.

The grammatical-historical commentators reconstruct the history. Barnes, Clarke and Robertson explain that Herodias had been wife to Herod Philip; Antipas, who had married a daughter of Aretas king of Arabia, took her, so that the union was at once adulterous and incestuous, forbidden by Leviticus 18:16. Robertson calls it ‘a nasty mess equal to any of our modern divorces.’ Ellicott and Robertson, citing Josephus, locate the prison at the fortress of Machaerus in Perea, and Robertson lingers on its dungeons overlooking the Dead Sea and Jordan. They cross-reference Mark 6:17 and Luke 3:19.

The Reformed Calvin weighs the sources carefully, arguing that the Evangelists give the true reason — Herod was offended at being reproved — against Josephus’s political explanation, and untangles the names (Philip, not the Tetrarch of Trachonitis). Calvin draws the pastoral lesson that God’s servants must be armed with unshaken fortitude before princes, where flattery is prevalent; John, ‘a disagreeable and unwelcome adviser,’ scrupled not to incur the tyrant’s frown. Gill notes only that Herod sent servants to seize him.

The Alexandrian Origen alone reads typologically: as the Law and the prophets were until John, so the Jewish rulers’ power of life and death lasted until John; when the last prophet was unlawfully killed, that power was withdrawn, so that afterward Pilate, not the Sanhedrin, condemned Jesus — a fulfilment, he thinks, of Jacob’s word that the sceptre should not depart from Judah (Genesis 49:10). Wesley adds simply that Philip was still living. The consensus is moral: John suffered for the truth of righteousness, reproving the great without flattery. Robertson, with Edersheim, adds that the windows of the fortress commanded a grand prospect of the Dead Sea, the Jordan and Jerusalem, while Calvin presses that the crime was ‘still recent’ and ‘committed before the eyes of all,’ so that the Evangelists’ plain account must be preferred to the political gloss of Josephus.
4:
‘It is not lawful for thee to have her’ — the witnesses dwell on John’s plain, courageous fidelity. Across schools the verse is read as the model of a prophet reproving a king to his face.

The grammatical-historical voices anchor the charge in the Law. Lightfoot cites the rabbinic list of ‘thirty-six cuttings off,’ among them lying with a brother’s wife, and notes that Philip was still alive into the twentieth year of Tiberius. Barnes points to Leviticus 18:16 and Leviticus 20:21. Robertson reads the imperfect (elegen) as meaning John said it repeatedly — ‘a blunt and brave thing’ that cost him his head, ‘but it is better to have a head like John’s and lose it than to have an ordinary head and keep it.’ Ellicott adds, from Josephus, that Antipas also feared a popular outbreak.

The Reformed Gill stresses John’s zeal for holiness, his hatred of sin, and his courage in reproving so great a man, showing that he came in the spirit of Elijah. The Wesleyan commentators make the verse a manual for ministers. Clarke holds up John’s ‘zeal, fidelity, and courage’ as worthy of imitation, with plainness and respect, and warns of the unutterable punishment of chaplains to the great who flatter their vices. Wesley notes that the marriage was unlawful for both parties — Herodias’s descent made it doubly so — and that ‘John’s words were rough, like his raiment’; he ‘would not break the force of truth by using soft words, even to a king.’

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is a catechetical excerpt on the dignity of the apostles above Elijah and the prophets, not a comment on this verse, and so stands apart from the others. The shared and dominant note remains plain: faithful reproof of sin owes no deference to rank, and John’s boldness is the pattern. Robertson, following Broadus, suggests the Pharisees may have inveigled John to Machaerus to draw out his opinion of the marriage, while Antipas curbed his own resentment through fear of a people who still held John a prophet; the dominant note remains that faithful reproof of sin owes no deference to a crown.
5:
Herod would have killed John but feared the multitude, who counted John a prophet. The witnesses agree that fear of man, not fear of God, restrained him — and several work to reconcile this with Mark’s portrait of a Herod who reverenced John.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Barnes ties the popular esteem of John to Matthew 21:26 and Luke 20:6. Ellicott, drawing on Mark’s fuller narrative, notes that Herod himself ‘feared John,’ knowing him a just and holy man, and ‘heard him gladly’ (Mark 6:20); there was ‘a struggle of conscience against passion,’ as in Ahab with Elijah, while Herodias, like Jezebel, thirsted for blood without halting. Clarke calls Herod a ‘miserable prince’ who fears the people more than he fears sinning against God.

The Reformed Calvin gives the closest analysis, resolving the apparent clash between the Evangelists: at first Herod was unwilling, restrained by reverence for a prophet of God; Herodias’s incessant urging wore that down; and then a new restraint, the dread of a popular commotion, held him back. Calvin distinguishes Herod’s voluntary respect for John from the forced reverence men give to the powerful — Herod was ‘convinced that he was a holy man.’ From this he draws the rule for pious teachers: never to wink at the faults of princes to purchase favour. Gill harmonises the same way, that Herod was hindered partly by conscience, partly by reverence, and especially by fear of the people.

The Reformed-grammatical Jamieson-Fausset-Brown entry here is misplaced, belonging to the sign-seeking of Matthew 12:38, and does not bear on the verse. Wesley sums the consensus: Herod would have killed John in a fit of passion, but was restrained first by fear of the multitude and afterward by the reverence he bore him. The shared lesson is that the wicked are often kept from sin not by godliness but by self-interest (compare Ecclesiastes 7:17). Gill adds that Herodias would gladly have killed John herself but for Herod, and Calvin observes that Mark’s phrase — that she ‘sought an opportunity’ — reveals her plotting against a husband not yet willing, so that two restraints, conscience and the fear of the people, successively held the tetrarch’s hand.
6:
Herod’s birthday feast and the dancing of Herodias’s daughter draw a broad response. The witnesses unite in condemning the occasion as the seedbed of the murder, and the fathers add an allegorical layer the moderns set aside.

The Latin and Greek fathers moralise the scene. Jerome notes that only Herod and Pharaoh (Genesis 40:20) are recorded keeping birthdays, ‘alike in wickedness, alike in festivities,’ and suspects Herod swore the oath on purpose; he recalls the Roman Flaminius expelled from the senate for mixing feasting with blood. Chrysostom (Antiochene) finds a twofold charge against the girl — that she danced and that she asked an execution as reward. Remigius blames a mother who taught her daughter dancing rather than chastity and turned a palace into a theatre. Gregory wonders that so great a man dies for ‘the sport of shameful men,’ concluding that God oppresses His people in little things to reward them in the highest. Isidore lays down that ‘an oath is not to be observed by which we have pledged ourselves to evil.’ Hilary reads the whole as figure: the dancing daughter is pleasure springing from unbelief, the head of John the glory of the Law surrendered to it.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the realities. Lightfoot reports that the Jewish schools reckoned birthday-keeping a Gentile, idolatrous custom, yet that festal dancing was their own practice. Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson explain that Salome’s public dancing before banqueters violated all eastern rules of female modesty; Ellicott likens Herodias bending Herod to Madame du Barry with Louis XV, and Robertson calls it ‘a shameful exhibition… prearranged by Herodias.’

The Reformed Calvin and Gill, with the Wesleyan Clarke and Wesley, agree that the lawful joy of a birthday was profaned by luxury: Calvin warns that ‘nothing is so pure that the world shall not taint it,’ recalling Job’s sacrifices for his feasting sons (Job 1:5), and that a marriageable girl’s dancing was ‘a shameful display.’ The dominant verdict is that unbridled mirth opened the door to murder. Lightfoot adds that festal leaping and dancing marked the Jews’ own rejoicings, even at the Feast of Tabernacles, so the scandal lay not in dancing as such but in a princess exposing herself before banqueters; Wesley notes the daughter became ‘infamous for a life suitable to this beginning,’ the seed of the feast bearing its bitter fruit.
7:
Herod’s oath to give the girl ‘whatsoever she would ask’ draws comment only from the grammatical-historical school here, and they converge on its rashness and folly. With so few and like-minded voices, the synthesis is correspondingly modest.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the oath as both sin and blunder. Barnes calls it ‘a foolish and wicked oath’: to please a wanton girl Herod called the eternal God to witness his willingness to give half his kingdom and even to shed holy blood — ‘an oath like this it was not lawful to make, and it should have been broken’ (he points forward to v.9). Lightfoot identifies the form as what the Talmudists called a ‘rash oath,’ and notes the grim symmetry: if Herod swore ‘by his head,’ the girl’s demand for a head answered the oath, ‘though very unjustly.’

Ellicott sets the promise against the lavish rewards the scandalous chronicles of the day recorded for dancers, suggesting Herod imagined himself reproducing the magnificence of the imperial court at Rome; he expected she would ask ‘a jewel, a bracelet, a palace, or a city’ — not, as Mark reports, that he had pledged ‘the half of his kingdom’ (Mark 6:23). Robertson notes the verb means literally ‘confessed with an oath,’ and that the middle voice of ‘ask’ means she asked for herself, comparing Esther’s being offered a request in Esther 5:3 and Esther 7:2.

Gill, with the same school’s temper, observes only that Herod was so charmed by the dancing — and by the pleasure it gave the whole court — that he made the promise. The unanimous note is that a promissory oath, sworn rashly to gratify the flesh, became the snare by which a murder was procured; the very generosity Herod meant as display became the instrument of Herodias’s revenge. Gill observes that Herod first promised and then bound the promise with an oath, so charmed was he by the dance and by the pleasure it gave the court; the witnesses thus see a double folly — an unbounded pledge and a needless oath — by which a vain man delivered himself into the hands of a scheming wife and daughter.
8:
The girl, ‘instructed of her mother,’ asks for John’s head in a charger. The witnesses are agreed on the horror of the request and on Herodias as its true author; they divide only on the small point of when the instruction was given.

The grammatical-historical commentators establish the facts. Barnes explains that the request came after the dance, on consulting her mother (Mark 6:24), and that the demand for a head on a platter followed the custom of rulers requiring proof that an enemy was dead — he cites the Roman Agrippina’s handling of Lollia Paulina’s head. Ellicott presses the timing: ‘being prompted’ (better than ‘instructed’) does not mean she knew what to ask before she danced; Mark says she went out to ask her mother, whose absence shows the supper was for men only. Robertson notes the verb ‘put forward’ (compare Acts 19:33) and Bruce’s remark that it would take much ‘educating’ to bring a girl to so grim a request; he also balks, like the others, at the obsolete word ‘charger’ for a platter.

The Wesleyan voices vent moral indignation. Clarke exclaims at ‘what an infernal mother’ and ‘what a promising daughter,’ calling the bloody head a monstrous present for a young lady. Wesley notes she was instructed both as to the matter and the manner — ‘Give me here’ — fearing that if Herod had time to consider he would not do it. The Reformed Gill agrees the daughter was hastened in by a mother ‘eager to satisfy her revenge.’

Barnes finds the one gleam of apparent propriety — the girl honouring her mother by consulting her — only deepens the guilt, since it was used to accomplish wickedness more effectually (compare Proverbs 29:10). The shared verdict is that the demand was Herodias’s, executed through a pliant child, and aimed at the tongue that had reproved her. Gill stresses that the girl went out to her mother and returned ‘straightway with haste,’ urged on lest the king’s mood should pass, so that the very speed of the demand betrays the long-laid malice behind it; the request was Herodias’s, aimed at the tongue that had reproved her, and the child was only its willing instrument.
9:
‘The king was sorry; nevertheless for the oath’s sake… he commanded it.’ The witnesses converge powerfully here: the oath was no excuse, and Herod’s real master was the fear of his guests’ opinion. The reflection on false honour is the chapter’s sharpest ethical thread.

The grammatical-historical commentators expose the false plea. Barnes lists why Herod was sorry — his respect for John, John’s standing with the people, and a conscience that still spoke — but insists Herod was not bound by the oath: a prior obligation, the law of God against murder, could not be dissolved by any oath. He then turns the verse into a denunciation of the ‘laws of honour’ and duelling: men murder because they fear ridicule from the wicked, and this is ‘the source of all youthful guilt’ (compare 1 Samuel 14:28, Ecclesiastes 5:2). Ellicott names it ‘the last struggle of conscience,’ defeated because, ‘like most weak men, Herod feared to be thought weak.’ Robertson, noting the word means ‘grieved,’ says men who do wrong ‘always have some flimsy excuses,’ and thinks Herod was ‘maudlin with wine.’

The Reformed Gill assembles Herod’s several sorrows — for John, for his own conscience, for the people, and even the Roman superstition against executions on a birthday. The Wesleyan Clarke notes the plural ‘oaths’ (one sin begets many) and that the guests, men like himself, would have counted a broken promise a breach of honour. Wesley distils the irony: ‘so he murdered an innocent man from mere tenderness of conscience.’

The dominant and shared conclusion is that no oath can sanctify a sin: Herod hid a real malice and a real cowardice behind a show of scruple, sacrificing John to save face before his table-companions. A point of honour, the witnesses agree, weighed more with him than a point of conscience. Barnes drives the point home that the same false fear of men’s opinion is, in his judgement, ‘the source of all youthful guilt,’ men being led into sin because they dare not follow conscience against the wicked; and the witnesses unite that no oath, however solemn, can sanctify the breaking of God’s prior and standing law against murder.
10:
‘He sent and beheaded John in the prison.’ The witnesses, chiefly the grammatical-historical school with the Wesleyans, treat the bare fact with restraint and turn to its setting, its timing, and the greatness of the man so meanly slain.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the details. Gill notes the executioner is named by a Latin term (speculator) used of those who carried out beheadings. Robertson observes the causative force — Herod ‘had John beheaded.’ Lightfoot raises a chronological problem: if John lay at Machaerus on the edge of Perea while Herod’s court was at Tiberias, the executioner’s journey and the presenting of the head must have taken some days; he ties the timing to the nearness of the Passover noted in John 6:4. Barnes cites Josephus that Herodias was ‘full of ambition and envy,’ able to persuade Herod to things against his inclination — a proof that the Evangelists drew characters ‘according to truth.’

Ellicott rises to eulogy. Measured by earthly greatness it seems a paradox to call a short-lived desert preacher one of the greatest of earth’s sons, ‘and yet this, and nothing less, if we accept our Lord’s words, must be our estimate’ (compare Matthew 11:11); he praises John’s intensity, courage, humility and self-denial, and the conviction that ‘the preaching of repentance must precede that of forgiveness.’

The Wesleyan Wesley voices the mystery of providence — that so holy a life was ‘sacrificed to the malice of an abandoned harlot, the petulancy of a vain girl, and the rashness of a foolish… prince’ — yet rests in the assurance that ‘the Almighty will repay his servants in another world for whatever they suffer in this.’ The shared note is sober: a martyr’s death, secretly executed, but the first New Testament minister falling, like the first Old Testament saint, as a martyr. Gill identifies the executioner by the Roman term speculator, an officer charged with such beheadings, and the witnesses agree the deed was done swiftly and in secret, the prison being close at hand; the first New Testament minister thus fell, like the first Old Testament saint, a true martyr, dying for the performance of his duty.
11:
The head is brought on a platter and given to the girl, who carries it to her mother. The witnesses, mostly grammatical-historical with a Wesleyan, dwell on the savagery of the scene and on the later fates of those who shared in it.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply detail and after-history. Gill infers from the speed that the prison was near, probably within the castle of Machaerus where the feast was held. Robertson paints ‘a gruesome picture as Herodias… witnesses the triumph of her implacable hatred,’ and rejects the later legends that made Salome in love with John as having no proof. Ellicott traces the sequel from Josephus: Herodias’s ambition later drove Antipas to seek a royal title, complaints from the Galileans followed them to Rome, and both were deposed and banished to Lugdunum (Lyons); he reports, as tradition, the gruesome account of Salome’s death on the ice.

The Wesleyan Clarke draws the minister’s warning: ‘there is no person so revengeful as a lascivious woman when reproved,’ and a preacher of the Gospel has most to fear from that quarter — ‘the first of this profession lost his life for the sake of truth and chastity.’ Barnes simply refers the reader back to v.6.

The Reformed-grammatical Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here in fact comments on Nineveh’s repentance and so does not bear on the verse. The dominant impression across the genuine voices is of cold-blooded malice satisfied: the trophy of John’s head passed from executioner to king to dancing girl to the woman whose sin he had reproved, while the providence of God is seen to overtake, in time, each accomplice in the deed. Barnes underscores that the head was given first to the girl and by her carried to her mother, so that the trophy passed through three guilty hands, while Robertson rejects as baseless the medieval legends that made Salome love John. Ellicott’s survey of the sequel — the deposition and banishment of Herod and Herodias, and the traditional manner of Salome’s death — he offers as the visible overtaking of guilt by providence, though he is careful to mark the death-legend as tradition rather than ascertained fact.
12:
John’s disciples bury his body and ‘go and tell Jesus.’ The witnesses read this both as the dignified close of John’s ministry and as the disciples’ turning to Christ; the moment also explains the withdrawal of v.13.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the significance. Barnes gives three reasons they came to Jesus: the event touched His own work, they sought consolation (‘it is not improper… to go and tell Jesus’), and they may have warned Him of danger (compare Acts 8:2). Robertson calls it ‘a shock to the Master who alone knew how great John really was,’ and ‘a prophecy of what was before Jesus,’ noting it prompted His retreat. Ellicott observes that John’s disciples now transferred their allegiance to their true Lord, though a remnant kept a separate existence, as the cases of Apollos (Acts 18:25) and the Ephesian disciples (Acts 19:3) show.

The Alexandrian Origen reads the withdrawal as a lesson in prudence: as far as it is in our power we are to withdraw from persecutors and expected conspiracies, following Christ’s own command, ‘if they persecute you in this city, flee to another’ (Matthew 10:23); yet a temptation we cannot avoid we must endure. He sees in the withdrawal ‘to a desert place’ a turning toward the Gentiles.

The Wesleyan Clarke turns sharply moral, repeating the report that Herodias pierced the dead tongue, and warning at length against ‘feasting and dancing’ as ‘snares for souls.’ The Reformed Gill notes the disciples likely sought leave to take the body, as Joseph of Arimathea would for Jesus’s. The shared and tender note is that when anything ails us it is our duty and privilege to make Christ acquainted with it, and that the loss of a faithful minister should draw believers nearer to the great Shepherd (Hebrews 13:20). Robertson observes that the disciples acted ‘as was meet’ in giving the body decent burial before coming to Jesus, and that the news led directly to His withdrawal — an added motive, with the need for rest, behind the retreat of the next verse; the loss of a faithful minister, the voices agree, should draw believers nearer to the great Shepherd.
13:
Jesus, hearing of John’s death, withdraws by ship to a desert place, and the crowds follow on foot. The witnesses agree the withdrawal was deliberate prudence, not fear, and that the people’s pursuit shows the magnetism of Christ; the fathers add a layer of allegory.

The Latin and Greek fathers probe the motive. Jerome insists He retired ‘not through fear of death… but in mercy to His enemies,’ to leave an example of shunning rashness — the Evangelist says not ‘fled’ but ‘withdrew’ (compare Matthew 10:23); the eager pursuit on foot shows the ardour of believing minds. Chrysostom (with the Catena and the separate Chrysostom entry, Antiochene) holds He withdrew to prolong ‘the economy of His humanity,’ confirming His true incarnation by acting as a man who waits to be told, yet the crowds, undeterred even by John’s end, follow and at once reap their reward in healing. Augustine fixes the chronology relative to v.1. Hilary reads it mystically: the Word, the Law being ended, enters the ship (the Church) and passes into the desert of Gentile hearts, the multitude moving from synagogue to Church.

The grammatical-historical commentators give geography and motive. Lightfoot locates the crossing from Capernaum to the desert of Bethsaida in the upper Gaulanitis, explaining how the crowd could follow on foot by the Jordan bridge. Barnes and Clarke agree He went to a place of safety, never throwing Himself needlessly into danger (compare Matthew 12:15, John 6:1). Ellicott adds two further causes from the other Gospels: rest for the returning Twelve (Mark 6:31) and the approaching Passover (John 6:4).

The Reformed Calvin and the Puritan Henry add the pastoral grain. Calvin reconciles the Synoptics (rest for the disciples and the shock of John’s death) and observes that of the vast crowd few truly adhered (John 6:66). Henry notes that ‘sometimes the sufferings of the saints are made to further the gospel’ (Philippians 1:12), and that Christ’s withdrawal only drew the people the more. Wesley gathers the threefold reason from the Gospels — to avoid Herod, to escape the pressing multitude, and to confer with the returning disciples — while Gill notes that Herod’s talk of Him as the risen Baptist was itself among the reports that moved Christ to retire; the withdrawal, all the genuine voices hold, was wisdom, not cowardice.
14:
Jesus goes forth, is moved with compassion, and heals the sick. The witnesses unite on the compassion of Christ as the spring of the miracle, the grammatical school stressing the word itself and the Reformed and Alexandrian voices drawing it toward the soul.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the compassion. Barnes and Clarke explain that He pitied them as ‘sheep having no shepherd’ (Mark 6:34), having no teachers who cared for them — the scribes being proud and neglectful — so they flocked to the one who preached good news to the poor (Matthew 11:5, John 10:14). Clarke and Robertson note the strong verb (esplanchnisthe), the ‘motion of the bowels’ that is the oriental seat of compassion (compare Matthew 9:36). Ellicott adds that, seeing the eager crowd, instead of retiring further He went forward to meet them.

The Reformed Calvin gives the fullest reading. The compassion was roused because He saw famishing souls whom zeal had led into the wilderness; as man He judged according to the present case, and as the appointed public teacher He counted all the Jews, for the time, as belonging to God’s flock. Though wearied and seeking rest, ‘when urgent duty calls him to additional labour, he willingly lays aside that private consideration’; and Calvin assures us that, now in heaven, He still ‘looks down on poor sheep that have no shepherd.’ He notes Mark’s emphasis that Christ first taught at length, healing being only part of the work.

The Alexandrian Origen turns the sicknesses inward: the lovers of money, ambition, and lust are the diseased of soul whom Christ in compassion heals, distinguishing (from 1 Corinthians 11:30) between sin and sickness of soul. Gill notes that Christ went forth from the ship or the mountain to the waiting company. The dominant, shared note is plain: the miracles flow from a real and human pity that does not even require faith of those it heals. Calvin adds that the same compassion still reaches the believing from heaven, for though Christ has laid aside the feelings proper to a mortal man, He ‘looks down on poor sheep that have no shepherd’ who ask relief; and the voices unite that the miracles sprang from a real and human pity which asked no faith of those it relieved.
15:
At evening the disciples ask Jesus to send the crowd away to buy food. The witnesses read this as the prelude to the feeding, and the fathers especially mine it for typology while the moderns supply the realities of the ‘two evenings’ and the disciples’ imperfect faith.

The Greek and Latin fathers read richly. Chrysostom (Antiochene) makes the desert place deliberate — to clear the coming miracle of all suspicion, so that none could say food was fetched from a nearby town; the crowd’s endurance of hunger proves their faith, and the Lord, who ‘always… waits to be asked,’ recalls the taunt ‘Can he spread a table in the wilderness?’ (Psalms 78:19). Jerome, Hilary and Raban read the five loaves as the five books of the Law and the two fishes as the Prophets (and Psalms), broken and multiplied to feed the Gentiles; Hilary has the people sit on the grass as resting on the Law. Remigius reads the evening as the close of the age, or the Lord’s death. Origen presses the ‘last hour’ (1 John 2:18), the disciples saying ‘the time is past’ as the season of the Law and prophets passing.

The grammatical-historical commentators give the setting. Gill, Barnes and Robertson explain that this was the first of the Jews’ two evenings (from about three o’clock), since a second evening follows; the place was lonely rather than desolate, with villages near enough to buy food but needing time. Ellicott calls this the only miracle in all four Gospels, ‘the fullest manifestation of the sovereignty of the Son of Man over… nature,’ and dismisses as ‘ludicrously inadequate’ the rationalising theory that the crowd merely shared hidden stores; he weaves in John’s detail of the two hundred pennyworth (John 6:7).

The Reformed Calvin notes Christ purposely delayed, that the disciples might attend more carefully and learn that He never ceases to care for His people though He does not always at once supply their wants. The dominant note is that the disciples’ helplessness sets the stage for the display of divine power and provision. Wesley notes simply that ‘the time is now past’ means the usual meal-time was over; the disciples’ sense of the hour and the place, the witnesses agree, only set the stage for a provision that no town could supply.
16:
‘They need not depart; give ye them to eat.’ The witnesses fasten on the astonishing command and on the lesson Christ means to teach His disciples: to act in faith where they see only impossibility.

The grammatical-historical commentators sharpen the command. Barnes introduces John’s detail that Jesus first proved Philip with ‘Whence shall we buy bread?’ (John 6:5), testing a faith that immediately reckoned two hundred pennyworth too little; Christ ‘requires us to do what he commands… and we need not doubt that he will give us strength.’ Robertson notes the emphatic ‘ye’ set against the disciples’ ‘send away,’ and the urgent aorist ‘give’ — ‘an astounding command,’ teaching that ‘no situation appears to Him desperate.’

The Reformed Calvin gives the doctrinal frame: hitherto Christ had fed souls, but now He takes the care of bodies too, confirming His own word that to those who seek the kingdom ‘all these things shall be added’ (Matthew 6:33); those who would have Christ for provider must ‘be satisfied with barley-bread.’ He notes too that Christ chose to work through the disciples’ hands, that His power should not merely be seen but ‘handled by them.’

The Wesleyan Clarke turns it to ministry: ‘he that seeks first the kingdom of heaven is sure to have every temporal requisite,’ and bids the feeble minister begin at Christ’s command — ‘divide what you have, and the bread of God shall be multiplied in your hands.’ The Alexandrian Origen reads the loaves as the words of Scripture (five for the senses) and the two fishes as the spoken and conceived word, which Christ extends as far as He wills; Gill notes Christ said it to try their faith. The shared lesson is that what is too little in human hands becomes sufficient when brought to Christ and dispensed at His word. Calvin presses that Christ chose to work through the disciples’ hands, that His power should be not merely seen by them but ‘handled by them,’ and that those who would have Christ for their provider must learn to be content with barley-bread; the shared lesson is that what is too little in human hands proves sufficient when brought to Christ and dispensed at His word.
17:
‘We have here but five loaves and two fishes.’ The few witnesses here, chiefly grammatical-historical, treat the disciples’ protest as honest but small-faithed, and add their characteristic notes of detail.

The grammatical-historical commentators set out the facts and the temper. Robertson observes that the disciples ‘are quick with reasons for their inability to perform the task imposed by Jesus.’ Barnes notes from John that the loaves were barley and in the keeping of a lad (John 6:9), barley being the cheap food of the poor, and fish a staple of a people living on a lake that ‘abounded in fish.’ Lightfoot, with his rabbinic interest, discusses whether the two fishes were ‘salt fish,’ comparing the Talmudic rules of blessing over salted food, and wonders how Christ’s manner of blessing would compare. Gill notes the disciples spoke to ‘excuse themselves, and to show the impossibility.’

The Wesleyan Clarke turns the smallness to spiritual use: ‘when we are deeply conscious of our own necessities, we shall be led to depend on Jesus with a firmer faith,’ for God often brings His servants low ‘that they may have repeated opportunities of proving the kindness… of their… Master.’

The Reformed-grammatical Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here in fact comments on Jesus’s relatives coming to seize Him (Mark 3:21), and so does not bear on the verse. The dominant and shared sense is that the disciples’ frank accounting of their poverty — five loaves and two fishes — both sets the scale of the miracle and exposes how easily faith reasons from what is seen rather than from the power of Christ. Barnes notes the fish were the ordinary fare of a lakeside people, and the witnesses agree that the very smallness of the store — five barley cakes and two fishes for thousands — is precisely what makes the ensuing miracle unmistakable, leaving the disciples, as Clarke observes, to learn dependence through their own conscious want. Their candid reckoning of their poverty thus both measures the wonder to come and exposes how readily faith calculates from what lies in hand rather than from the boundless power of Christ.
18:
‘Bring them hither to me.’ The brief verse draws a short but pointed comment from a handful of voices, all reading it as the turning-point where the disciples’ poverty is put into Christ’s hands.

The grammatical-historical Robertson catches the contrast sharply: here is ‘the helpless doubt of the disciples and the confident courage of Jesus.’ The very ‘five loaves and two fishes’ the disciples had named as a reason for doing nothing, Christ takes up as the means of doing everything — ‘they had overlooked the power of Jesus in this emergency.’

The Reformed Gill draws three motives from the command: it signified that the little was sufficient, or would be made so; and it was meant ‘to try their faith in him, their obedience to him, and their liberality to others.’ He adds the practical maxim that ‘the best way to have an increase of temporal supplies is to bring what we have, and put it into Christ’s hands,’ so that good is done to others and returned with overplus to the giver.

The Wesleyan Clarke generalises the lesson: ‘no creature of God should be considered as good or safe without the blessing of God in it’; even ‘a handful of meal and a few herbs,’ brought to Christ ‘by prayer and faith,’ become ‘a sufficiency for thy body, and a sacrament to thy soul.’ He presses it on the minister, whose word ‘can be of no use, unless the blessing of Christ be in it.’ Barnes simply refers back to v.13. The shared, modest note across these voices is that surrender of our scanty resources to Christ is the condition of their increase: what is brought to Him He blesses, multiplies, and returns. Robertson stresses that the very provisions named as a reason for inaction become, in Christ’s hands, the means of feeding thousands, the disciples having ‘overlooked the power of Jesus in this emergency’; and Gill’s maxim — that bringing what we have to Christ is the way to see it increased and returned to the giver with overplus — gathers the practical sense of all three voices.
19:
Christ seats the multitude, looks up to heaven, blesses, breaks, and gives. The witnesses agree on the orderly arrangement, on the giving of thanks as the model for our meals, and — with the grammatical school and Ellicott — on the eucharistic foreshadowing.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the scene and the blessing. Barnes and Robertson note the people reclined on the grass in companies (Mark and Luke), making them easy to count and feed, and explain that ‘to bless’ here means to give thanks, citing the customary Jewish grace, ‘Blessed art thou… who bringest bread out of the earth.’ Clarke records the Hebrew forms of the blessing and notes that bread was ‘broken,’ not cut, being made in thin cakes. Robertson, defending the nature-miracle, urges that if Jesus is Lord of the universe (John 1:1, Colossians 1:16) ‘why should we balk at this miracle?’ Ellicott confesses the process is ‘inconceivable in its details’ — the Evangelists ‘did not care to ask’ — yet sees in the act, with its later echo at the Last Supper, the foreshadowing that Christ would ‘impart to souls… the gift of communion with Himself,’ the true Bread from heaven.

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson that ‘we cannot partake of our food with holiness… unless we express our gratitude to God,’ citing that every creature is sanctified by thanksgiving (1 Timothy 4:5); the looking up to heaven is ‘an excitement well fitted to arouse us from sloth,’ not a posture required at all times. Gill notes the disciples ranged the people in companies so the food might be orderly distributed.

The shared note is that the Creator’s power is exercised through ordinary forms — thanksgiving, breaking, the disciples’ hands — and that the miracle teaches both devotion at our tables and the deeper truth that Christ Himself is the bread of life. Clarke records the Hebrew forms of the Jewish grace before and after meat, and the witnesses join devotion at the table to the deeper truth, pressed by Ellicott, that the act foreshadowed the giving of Himself as the true Bread from heaven; the Creator’s power is exercised through the ordinary forms of thanksgiving, breaking, and the disciples’ hands.
20:
‘They did all eat, and were filled,’ and twelve baskets of fragments remained. The witnesses unite that this proves a true creative miracle and that the gathered surplus both magnifies it and teaches thrift.

The grammatical-historical commentators press the proof and the detail. Barnes calls it ‘an undoubted miracle’ — to multiply so much ‘had the power of creation’ and so ‘no less than Divine power’ — and explains that the baskets were the travellers’ provision-baskets carried by Jews journeying among Gentiles, citing John’s ‘that nothing be lost’ (John 6:12) as ‘an example of economy.’ Clarke insists there was ‘a manifest creation of substance… attested by at least five thousand,’ and, with Lightfoot’s rabbinic note that being ‘filled’ meant truly satisfied, observes that the Jews’ common basket (the cophinus) was proverbial — he and Robertson both quote Juvenal’s Jews whose furniture was ‘a basket and some hay.’ Robertson reckons each of the twelve had a basketful left over, and hopes the lad of John 6:9 got one. Ellicott suggests the fragments were reserved for distribution to the poor.

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine: the leftovers, ‘more than twelve times larger… than what was at first put into their hands,’ showed the power created not only for immediate use but for future want. He extends it to providence — that the annual increase of corn is the same blessing of God, unseen only because ‘our own indolence and ingratitude’ blind us, and that ‘the food which we eat proceeds from his grace.’ Gill notes simply that everyone had their portion.

The dominant, shared note is that the abundance — full satisfaction and an overflowing surplus — at once seals the miracle as creative, displays the plenteousness of Christ’s provision, and rebukes waste, since what God gives is sacred and none should be lost. Lightfoot’s rabbinic note that to be ‘filled’ meant truly satisfied, and the shared observation that the surplus far exceeded the original store, both serve the one end — to seal the feeding as a creative work and to display the plenteousness of Christ’s provision, which rebukes all waste, since what God gives is sacred and none should be lost.
21:
‘They that had eaten were about five thousand men, beside women and children.’ The witnesses use the number to magnify the miracle and to settle a small ambiguity in the counting; the Alexandrian voice alone allegorises.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the figure. Gill notes the large number fed ‘with five loaves and two fishes,’ remarking that some versions drop ‘about.’ Barnes reckons the total, counting women and children, might have been ten thousand, calls it ‘an act of great benevolence, and a stupendous miracle,’ and links it to John’s report that the people thereupon judged Jesus ‘that prophet’ — the Messiah (John 6:14). Ellicott and Robertson observe that all four Gospels use the word that emphasises the five thousand were men; the women and children, fewer because many had come far, were grouped apart and not counted, ‘so that the round number dwelt in men’s minds.’ Robertson is careful to add this ‘does not mean that the women and children were not fed.’

The Alexandrian Origen takes up the ‘ambiguous expression,’ asking whether the five thousand were the only eaters or only the men counted. He proposes that the worthy, ‘corresponding to the men of twenty years old’ numbered in the book of Numbers, were reckoned, while the less worthy — figured as ‘children’ and ‘women’ in the sense of 1 Corinthians 3:1 — were not, reading the categories allegorically of spiritual maturity.

The shared, dominant note is that the bare count — thousands fed from a few loaves — is itself the argument: a benevolence and a creative power that left no doubt, and convinced the crowd, however imperfectly, that the long-expected Prophet had come. Barnes reckons that, with the uncounted women and children, the company may have reached ten thousand, and the witnesses are careful that the counting of ‘men’ does not imply the women and children went unfed, only that the round number lodged in memory. The bare arithmetic — thousands satisfied from five loaves — is itself the argument for a creative power that left no honest room for doubt, and convinced the crowd, however imperfectly, that the promised Prophet had come.
22:
This long verse opens the second miracle: Jesus ‘constrains’ the disciples into the ship and sends the crowd away. The witnesses agree the compulsion was urgent and purposeful, and most, following John, tie it to the crowd’s attempt to make Him king; the fathers add a sweep of allegory over the whole storm-narrative.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the force. Robertson and Ellicott note the strong verb ‘compelled,’ explained by John’s account that the people meant to take Him by force and make Him king (John 6:15); this would have been a political revolution defeating Christ’s purpose, and even the disciples were ‘swept off their feet by the mob psychology,’ sharing the hope of a political kingdom. Barnes and Clarke add the geography — they were sent toward Bethsaida and Capernaum (Mark 6:45) — and Barnes draws the example of retiring alone to pray when human honours press. Lightfoot agrees the compulsion was lest the disciples conspire in the same king-making.

The Latin and Greek fathers read the storm as figure. Chrysostom (Antiochene) holds He separated the disciples so the miracle of the loaves could be examined in His absence, and let them be tossed all night to train them to bear trials and to long for Him; Peter’s boldness and faltering teach that ‘not the tempest but the smallness of his faith’ endangered him. Augustine and Hilary read the ship as the Church tossed by the world’s persecutions, kept from sinking because Christ prays; the four watches are the ages of Law, Prophets, the Incarnation, and His return in glory, the ‘fourth watch’ His coming at the end. Jerome turns the Marcionite objection: if Christ’s body were a phantasm, how did Peter, confessedly a man, walk? Raban defends His true bodily weight against Theodore.

The Reformed Calvin and the Puritan Henry draw the obedience and the comfort: Calvin praises the disciples’ submission in leaving Him against their own judgement — ‘we do not truly… obey God, unless we… follow whatever he commands, though our feelings may be opposed’; Henry observes that they met the storm ‘where Christ sent them,’ for it is ‘no new thing for Christ’s disciples to meet with storms in the way of their duty.’ The shared note is that Christ orders His own into trial for their training, withdrawing to pray while never abandoning them.
23:
‘He went up into a mountain apart to pray… he was there alone.’ The witnesses dwell with one voice on Christ’s example of solitary prayer, and on the meaning of His praying as the God-man.

The Antiochene Chrysostom (the John Chrysostom entry) makes the mountain a lesson that ‘loneliness and retirement is good when we are to pray to God’: the wilderness is ‘the mother of quiet… a calm and a harbour.’ He sees Christ again withdrawing while the disciples are left in the storm without Him — ‘gently and by degrees He… urges them on… even to the bearing all nobly’ — casting them into longing and remembrance of Him.

The grammatical-historical commentators add place and motive. Robertson observes that after the dismissal Jesus went up alone, for ‘if ever he needed the Father’s sympathy, it was now’: the masses wild with enthusiasm, the disciples misunderstanding Him, ‘the Father alone could offer help now.’ Lightfoot clarifies that this ‘evening’ means the night, citing the rabbinic reckoning. Clarke draws the rule that one employed in mercy should return as quickly as possible to prayer, lest he value himself for work in which he has no merit, and lists the conditions of praying well — retirement, elevation of heart, solitude, the quiet of night.

The Reformed Calvin gives the doctrinal balance. Christ, though aware of the coming tempest, ‘showed himself to be God and man… by praying’; this was not hypocrisy but ‘sincere and human affection towards us.’ Solitude has ‘a powerful influence’ in disposing us to pour out the heart before God as our only witness; yet Calvin guards against a fixed rule — Paul bids men pray everywhere (1 Timothy 2:8), and Christ Himself prayed before others and taught social prayer, so the freedom to pray in all places does not bar secret prayer at proper seasons. Gill notes the people had not wholly dispersed but waited, and later took shipping to Capernaum. The shared note is the pattern of the Lord at prayer — withdrawn, long, and alone with the Father. Gill notes that the crowds had not wholly dispersed but waited, later taking ship to Capernaum; the witnesses unite in setting before us the pattern of the Lord at prayer — withdrawn, prolonged, and alone with the Father in the night.
24:
The ship is in the midst of the sea, tossed with waves, the wind contrary. The witnesses are chiefly grammatical-historical with Calvin, and they read the distress as both a real peril and a test of faith; one fault, the loaves so soon forgotten, is pressed by the Reformed.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the realities. Gill, citing John, places the ship some twenty-five furlongs out; Barnes computes about four miles, ‘literally in the midst of the sea,’ the lake being at most ten miles broad. Clarke notes the verb ‘tossed’ means grievously agitated, with a variant reading ‘plunged under the waves,’ and Robertson sees the boat ‘bobbing up and down in the choppy sea’ (Mark 6:48). Wesley adds the note on the two evenings.

The Reformed Calvin gives the fullest reading and the rebuke. Christ let them be tossed from near midnight till the fourth watch to fix their attention on the help He brought; their arms were ‘not more fatigued by rowing than their faith was shaken.’ The chief charge, with Mark, is that they had so soon let the miracle of the loaves fade — ‘they considered not the loaves’ — for ‘their heart was hardened’; Calvin makes this a warning about the corruption of our understanding, that we need new eyes from above (compare Deuteronomy 29:4).

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is a catechetical passage on the firmament and the waters above the heavens (Genesis 1:6), praising the Creator’s wisdom; it does not comment on the verse but stands, loosely, under the theme of God’s mastery over the waters. The shared and dominant note is that the storm was permitted, that the disciples’ extremity might prepare them for the help of Christ — and that their failure to read the recent miracle aright was the root of their alarm. Robertson pictures the boat ‘bobbing up and down in the choppy sea,’ and Calvin makes the disciples’ failure to read the recent miracle the very root of their alarm — a warning that we too need new eyes from above to perceive the works of God plainly set before us, lest a hardened heart leave us trembling where we ought to trust.
25:
‘In the fourth watch of the night he came to them, walking on the sea.’ The witnesses, almost all grammatical-historical, spend their effort on the time-reckoning and on defending the miracle as a true exercise of divine power.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the watch. Barnes, Clarke and Wesley set out that the Jews anciently kept three night-watches but had adopted the Roman four — the fourth running from three to six in the morning — so that Christ had spent most of the night alone in prayer before coming between three and six. Lightfoot, with characteristic precision, argues the Jews counted the fourth as morning rather than deep night, harmonising the ‘middle watch’ of Judges 7:19. The older watches are traced to Lamentations 2:19 and Exodus 14:24.

On the walking itself the school is emphatic. Clarke finds in the one act three wonders — that Christ knew their distress at a distance, found them in the dark, and walked the water — and points to Job 9:8, that God ‘treadeth upon the waves of the sea,’ concluding ‘Jesus did this by his own power; therefore Jesus showed forth his Godhead.’ Barnes calls it ‘a manifest and wonderful miracle’ on a boisterous sea in a dark night. Ellicott, guarding against minimising explanations (that He walked the shore, or swam), and noting John’s detail of the distance (John 6:19), sees an anticipation of the spiritual body of the Transfiguration and the Resurrection, while observing the disciples raised no such speculations but took Him for a phantom.

Robertson notes the grammar of ‘upon the sea’ and dismisses the attempt to explain it away as Jesus merely walking the beach as ‘an impossible theory unless Matthew’s account is legendary.’ Gill simply marks the watch. The shared, dominant note is that the walking on the water is an undisguised display of the Creator’s sovereignty over nature, wrought at the hour of the disciples’ deepest extremity. Robertson rejects the attempt to reduce the scene to Jesus merely walking the beach as ‘an impossible theory unless Matthew’s account is legendary,’ and Gill simply marks the watch; the genuine voices unite that the walking on the water is an undisguised display of the Creator’s sovereignty, wrought at the hour of the disciples’ deepest extremity.
26:
The disciples, seeing Him walk on the sea, are troubled and cry out, ‘It is a spirit.’ The few witnesses here, grammatical-historical, read their terror as natural fright deepened by the superstition of the age.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the fear. Gill supplies the conditions — early morning, perhaps moonlight, more light on the water than on land, yet not enough to distinguish who it was, and no expectation of seeing Christ — so that the sight of a man walking on the sea was ‘unusual and astonishing.’ Barnes notes ‘it was a common belief among the ancients that the spirits of men after death frequently appeared to the living’ (compare Luke 24:37, Job 9:8). Robertson says they were ‘literally terrified,’ the word ‘apparition’ (phantasma) meaning a ghost or spectre, and quotes Bruce’s ‘little touch of sailor superstition.’

The Wesleyan Clarke takes the occasion to defend the belief itself, asserting that ‘the spirits of the dead might and did appear’ was a doctrine held ‘by the greatest and holiest of men,’ which cavillers have never disproved — a characteristic aside rather than an exegesis of the verse.

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is again a catechetical passage, this time on the Holy Spirit and the new birth (John 3:5, Matthew 12:28), and so does not bear on the verse. The shared, dominant note across the genuine voices is that the disciples’ fright is wholly intelligible — the dark, the storm, the unlooked-for figure — and that their cry ‘it is a spirit’ sets the stage for the reassuring word of v.27. Gill dwells on the half-light of early morning — more light on the water than on land, yet too little to discern who walked there — so that men with no thought of Christ took the figure for a ghost. Barnes and Robertson agree the terror was the natural product of the dark, the storm, and the superstition of the age, the very word for ‘apparition’ meaning a spectre; the disciples’ fright is thus wholly intelligible, and only sets in relief the reassurance Christ is about to speak.
27:
‘Be of good cheer; it is I; be not afraid.’ The witnesses unite that this word of Christ, more than any deliverance, gave the disciples courage, and several hear in it a lasting watchword for the Church in distress.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the word’s power. Clarke holds that ‘nothing but this voice of Christ could, in such circumstances, have given courage and comfort,’ and that those grievously tossed with temptations need a like manifestation of His power and goodness — ‘when he proclaims himself in the soul, all sorrow, and fear, and sin are at an end.’ Ellicott marks the accuracy with which all three Evangelists preserve the words, a sign of the impression they made, and reads them as having become, for the Church at large, ‘something of a symbolic character’: often the sky darkens and the waves rise, and then ‘in the dim twilight they would see… the tokens of His presence’ — ‘Be of good cheer’ the watchword of their lives.

The Reformed Calvin gives the doctrinal balance. Because Christ is not known as a Deliverer till He appears, He speaks and bids them recognise Him; the confidence to which He calls them is ‘founded on his presence.’ Yet, terror having overpowered them, He first corrects the fear lest it hinder confidence — not that they could at once lay aside fear, but that it might not destroy faith. Calvin notes that the same voice and presence deadly to the reprobate work ‘inward peace and strong confidence’ in believers, and that we are needlessly disturbed because our ingratitude keeps us from using God’s gifts as shields.

Gill adds that Christ spoke ‘straightway… the very moment’ their fear arose, ‘as one truly affected towards them,’ coming not to frighten but to save, and Barnes refers to the same boldness given elsewhere (Acts 23:11). The shared, dominant note is that Christ’s self-revelation — ‘it is I’ — is the ground of all the disciples’ courage, turning terror into peace by His mere presence. Gill adds that Christ called out aloud the very moment their terror arose, as one truly moved for their good, coming not to frighten but to save; the witnesses unite that His self-revelation — ‘it is I’ — is the ground of all the disciples’ courage, turning terror into peace by His mere presence.
28:
‘Lord, if it be thou, bid me come unto thee on the water.’ The witnesses read Peter’s request as springing from genuine love yet from a faith not yet settled, and they weigh both the ardour and the rashness.

The grammatical-historical commentators size up Peter. Barnes finds here ‘the characteristic ardour and rashness of Peter’: he had ‘less real faith than he supposed; more ardour than his faith would justify’ — really attached to Jesus, yet easily daunted and prone to fall, so that he was suffered to learn his own character ‘by dear-bought experience.’ Robertson notes ‘the impulsiveness of Peter… as usual,’ and that Matthew alone records the episode. Clarke commends the better side: ‘to take Christ at his word argues not only the perfection of faith, but… the highest exercise of sound reason,’ for He is the Truth and can neither lie nor deceive.

The Reformed Calvin gives the closest analysis, holding that the very condition ‘if it be thou’ shows Peter’s faith ‘not yet fully settled’: having heard Christ speak, he still argues under doubt, and rather than pray for an increase of faith to walk by Christ’s direction, ‘without the wings of faith, he desires to fly at will.’ Calvin grants the wish sprang from a good principle but warns it degenerated into ‘a faulty excess’; let believers, instructed by his example, ‘beware of excessive haste’ — running gladly where the Lord calls, but never overleaping the bounds He prescribes.

Gill notes that Peter, knowing Christ’s voice and being ‘more forward than the rest,’ ventured to speak; Barnes adds the cross-reference to Paul’s confidence in Christ’s strength (Philippians 4:13). The shared, dominant note is a measured one: Peter’s desire to come to Christ is true love, and his asking leave shows due regard for Christ’s will, yet his faith is weak enough that the adventure will soon expose it — as v.30 will show. Gill notes that Peter, knowing his Master’s voice and bolder than the rest, ventured to speak, and Barnes draws the cross-reference to Paul’s confidence that he can do all things through Christ who strengthens him; the dominant note is measured — true love desiring to come to Christ, yet a faith still weak enough that the adventure will soon expose it.
29:
‘And he said, Come. And Peter… walked on the water, to go to Jesus.’ Only three voices comment, all briefly, and they read the episode as a real participation in Christ’s power granted to faith — with a caution against presuming on it.

The Reformed Gill explains why Christ said ‘Come’: partly to assure them who He was — ‘had he denied him, he and the rest might have concluded it was none of Jesus’ — and partly ‘to commend his love, and confirm his faith, by giving a further instance of his power’ in enabling Peter to walk upon the water as He did. The granting of the request, on this reading, is itself a sign meant to deepen the disciples’ confidence in their Lord.

The grammatical-historical Barnes simply refers the reader back to v.28, continuing the assessment of Peter’s character there. The Wesleyan Clarke draws the practical and cautionary lesson: ‘however impossible the thing commanded by Christ may appear, it is certain he will give power to accomplish it to those who receive his word by faith’; but he adds the warning ‘we must take care never to put Christ’s power to the proof for the gratification of a vain curiosity’ — or even to strengthen our faith, when the ordinary means of doing so are within reach.

With so few and so brief comments the synthesis is necessarily short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: at Christ’s single word Peter does what nature forbids, the miracle proving both the reality of Christ’s enabling power and that such power is given to obedient faith — never to be demanded for display. The walking itself is the brief, bright emblem of the believer borne up above the world while he keeps his eye on Christ, before the faltering of the next verse. Gill presses that Christ’s single word ‘Come’ both proved His identity and confirmed the disciples’ faith by a further instance of His power, and Clarke that such power to do the impossible is given to those who receive Christ’s word by faith — yet never to be demanded for the gratification of curiosity or to manufacture assurance when ordinary means are at hand.
30:
‘But when he saw the wind boisterous, he was afraid; and beginning to sink, he cried, Lord, save me.’ The witnesses agree that Peter’s sinking came from looking away from Christ to the danger — the failure of faith, not the violence of the storm.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the cause. Clarke states it plainly: ‘it was by faith in the power of Christ he was upheld; when that faith failed… no wonder that… he began to sink’ — ‘it was not the violence of the winds… but his littleness of faith.’ Ellicott describes ‘the conflict between sight and faith,’ faith worsted and the supernatural strength leaving him, so that the swimmer’s art could not save and he cried out in agony, met by his Lord’s ‘firm sustaining grasp.’ Robertson quotes Bruce — ‘it is one thing to see a storm from the deck… another to see it in the midst of the waves’ — and notes the aorist ‘save me’ means save quickly. Gill marks the wind blowing hard and the waves beating violently upon him.

The Wesleyan Wesley draws the same lesson with a psychological turn: though Peter ‘had been used to the sea, and was a skilful swimmer… when grace begins to act, the natural courage and strength are withdrawn’ — so that he must depend on Christ, not on himself.

The shared, dominant note is that Peter’s peril is the believer’s parable: so long as the eye is fixed on Christ and His word, the waters bear him; the moment he looks at the wind and waves, he begins to sink. Yet even the failing of faith does not leave him to perish, for it drives him to the right remedy — the cry, ‘Lord, save me’ (compare Lamentations 3:57) — which the next verse answers. Gill marks the wind blowing hard and the waves beating violently upon him, and the witnesses agree that Peter’s peril is the believer’s parable: while the eye is fixed on Christ the waters bear him, but the moment he looks at the storm he begins to sink, and yet even the failing of faith does not leave him to perish but drives him to the right resort.
31:
‘Immediately Jesus stretched forth his hand… O thou of little faith, wherefore didst thou doubt?’ The witnesses unite that Christ’s swift rescue is matched by a genuine reproof, and they weigh the kind of doubt that opposes faith.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle rescue and rebuke. Gill and Robertson note the immediacy — Christ, ‘just by him,’ took hold of Peter and pulled him up while still walking on the water — and Robertson glosses the rare verb ‘doubt’ (edistasas, from ‘twice’) as being ‘pulled two ways’: Peter’s trust gave way to his dread of the wind and waves. Clarke draws the constant lesson — ‘every moment we stand in need of Christ’ — bidding us look less at the danger than at the power of Christ by which we are upheld (compare Isaiah 53:12); Barnes points to the same caution against doubting (James 1:6).

The Reformed Calvin presses the question whether every fear betrays weak faith, and answers that Christ reproves ‘that kind of doubt which was directly opposed to faith’: a man may sometimes doubt without fault, where the word does not speak with certainty, but Peter, having an express command and recent experience of Christ’s power, abandoned that twofold support and fell into ‘foolish and wicked fear.’

The Antiochene Cyril of Jerusalem here comments on point, for once: ‘so great is the strength of faith, as even to buoy men up in walking on the sea’ — Peter, a man of flesh like us, found his faith ‘safer upon the waters than any ground,’ his heavy body upheld by faith’s buoyancy; but as his faith relaxed, his body was drawn down, and Jesus, ‘who remedies the distresses of our souls,’ reproved his doubt. The shared, dominant note is that Christ’s hand never fails the sinking believer, yet His love does not flatter the weakness of faith but names and rebukes it. Clarke draws the abiding lesson that every moment we stand in need of Christ, upheld while we stand by His power and saved when we fall by His mercy, bidding us look less at the danger than at the hand that holds us; the witnesses unite that Christ’s grasp never fails the sinking believer, though His love does not flatter the weakness of faith but names and rebukes it.
32:
‘And when they were come into the ship, the wind ceased.’ The witnesses, grammatical-historical with the Reformed Calvin echoed through the others, read the sudden calm as a fresh proof of Christ’s power and recall the disciples’ slowness to learn it.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the proof. Barnes calls it ‘a new proof of the power of Jesus’ — He who has power over winds and waves ‘has all power’ — and adds John’s detail that the ship was ‘immediately… at the land whither they went’ (John 6:21), ‘another proof… that the Son of God was with them.’ Robertson notes the verb ‘ceased’ comes from ‘toil’ — the wind ‘grew weary… in the presence of its Master’ — and that this was ‘not a mere coincidence’ (compare Mark 4:39). Clarke puts it devotionally: ‘Jesus is the Prince of peace, and all is peace and calm where he condescends to enter and abide.’

Ellicott draws out Mark’s sequel, that the disciples ‘were above measure astonished’ because such squalls usually died gradually and left the waves rough, whereas here the wind ceased in a moment as their Lord entered; and he records Mark’s reason for their astonishment — ‘they reflected not on the loaves, for their heart was hardened’ — so that, had they grasped the creative power of the feeding, nothing afterward would have seemed startling. Gill notes that, though Peter came in too, the calming is ascribed to Christ’s coming aboard, not to Peter’s.

The shared, dominant note is that the instant calm crowns the night’s wonders: the same Lord who walked the sea now stills it at His coming, and the disciples’ amazement only exposes how dull they had been to the lesson of the loaves — that He is sovereign over all nature. Ellicott notes from Mark that the disciples were ‘above measure astonished,’ since such squalls usually died slowly and left the waves rough, whereas here the wind ceased in a moment as their Lord came aboard; their amazement, he adds, only betrayed how dull they had been to the creative lesson of the loaves, their hearts being hardened.
33:
‘Then they that were in the ship… worshipped him, saying, Of a truth thou art the Son of God.’ The witnesses agree this is the night’s climax — a confession of Christ’s divine sonship — though they differ on who made it and on its full force.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the confessors and the title. Ellicott and Clarke think the words came chiefly from the crew or chance passengers distinct from Peter and the disciples — those ‘who had no previous knowledge’ of Christ — who in that moment of wonder ‘anticipated… the faith even of the foremost,’ Peter’s own confession (John 6:69) following shortly. Clarke argues from the Greek that the disciples had long since believed Him the Messiah; what was new was a fuller apprehension. Robertson notes Jesus ‘accepted’ the worship, that they were growing from the attitude of Matthew 8:27 toward the great confession of Matthew 16:16, and that the anarthrous form could mean ‘a Son of God’ (as the centurion in Matthew 27:54) though they probably meant more.

The Reformed Calvin takes the confessors to be sailors and passengers as well as disciples, who ‘instantly acknowledge that he is the Son of God,’ and by that term render Him the honour of the Messiah, since the prophets had taught that the Redeemer would be so called. The Alexandrian Origen restricts the confession to the disciples, noting they said not simply ‘Thou art the Son of God,’ as the demoniacs did (Matthew 8:29), but ‘Of a truth’ — a firmer faith — and reads the crossing-over allegorically as the passage to the eternal land.

Gill includes the mariners and owners among the worshippers. The shared, dominant note is that the double miracle — the walking and the calm — drew from those aboard a true act of worship and the chapter’s highest title: none but the world’s Creator could multiply the loaves, none but its Governor tread and still the sea; therefore ‘of a truth’ He is the Son of God. Clarke argues from the Greek that the title here marks a divine person, the Messiah whom the prophets foretold, so that the confession crowns the night’s two miracles; none but the world’s Creator could multiply the loaves, none but its Governor tread and still the sea, and therefore ‘of a truth’ He is the Son of God.
34:
They cross over and come to the land of Gennesaret. The witnesses read this transitional verse both for its geography and, among the fathers, for its figure of the Church brought safe to harbour; it opens the brief account of wholesale healings.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the place. Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott and Robertson identify Gennesaret as the rich, populous plain on the western shore that gave the lake one of its names, including Capernaum, toward which the disciples were steering (John 6:24, Mark 6:53). Robertson describes ‘a rich plain four miles long and two broad,’ with the people eager to touch the hem of His mantle as the woman did in Matthew 9:20.

The Latin and Greek fathers read it typologically. Chrysostom (Antiochene) marks the people’s greater faith: they no longer drag Him to their houses but bring all their sick to touch His garment, taught by the woman with the issue of blood — and he adds that we have not the hem only but Christ’s very body to receive. Jerome, Raban and Remigius play on the name (which they take as ‘a breeze’ or ‘beginning’): Christ guides the Church, delivered from the wreck of persecution, into a tranquil harbour, and the ‘hem’ is His least commandment or His assumption of a body. Hilary reads the people meeting Him as the believers saved out of the Law by faith, the virtue of the hem as the grace of the Spirit going out through the apostles.

The Reformed Calvin and the Puritan Henry draw the pastoral note. Calvin sees the country ‘filled with… proofs’ of Christ’s glory, making the nation’s later unbelief the more inexcusable, and finds the reason of His coming in Isaiah 53:4 — that in healing bodies He shadowed the healing of souls. Henry contrasts these eager Gennesarenes, who sought Christ, with the Gergesenes who begged Him to depart, and commends bringing others to the great Physician. The shared note is that crossing to Gennesaret brings Christ to a believing people who flock to His healing power. All the genuine voices unite that the crossing brings Christ to a believing and eager people, who no longer merely report Him but flock with their sick to His healing power; the fathers see in the safe arrival a figure of the Church brought through the storm of persecution into a tranquil harbour.
35:
‘When the men of that place had knowledge of him, they sent into all that country.’ The few witnesses here, grammatical-historical with a Wesleyan, read the verse as the spread of faith — those who knew Christ bringing others to Him.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the recognition and its effect. Gill says the men of Gennesaret knew Him ‘not merely by report, but by face,’ having seen and heard Him before. Clarke explains ‘they knew him again’: having already witnessed His miracles, they now ‘collect all the diseased people they can find,’ that He might show forth the same power and they be the instruments of relieving their neighbours; he turns it to application — let a man come to Jesus himself, and then ‘recommend this Christ to the whole circle of his acquaintance.’ Ellicott connects the place with the synagogue of Capernaum, where, meeting those who had seen the miracle of the loaves, Christ would lead them into the higher teaching of John 6:22 — the manifestation of divine power coinciding with divine wisdom in the new teaching.

The Wesleyan Wesley simply cross-refers to Mark 6:45. The Reformed-grammatical Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here in fact comments on Christ’s use of parables (Mark 4:10) and so does not bear on the verse.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: the people’s knowledge of Christ moved them at once to evangelistic diligence, sending word through all the surrounding country so that the sick might be brought to Him. The discerning of the day of opportunity, Henry’s neighbours showed, is the first step toward improving it — and those who have found the great Physician should spread the news that others may share the cure. Clarke turns the verse to application — let a man first come to Christ himself and then recommend Him to the whole circle of his acquaintance — and Ellicott connects the place with the synagogue of Capernaum, where Christ would lead those who had seen the loaves into the higher teaching of the discourse on the Bread of life; the people’s knowledge of Christ moved them at once to evangelistic diligence.
36:
They beseech Him that they might only touch the hem of His garment, ‘and as many as touched were made perfectly whole.’ The witnesses, grammatical-historical here, read the verse as the perfection of Christ’s healing met by a humble, importunate faith — though one notes the touch of superstition in it.

The grammatical-historical commentators set out the practice and its warrant. Gill explains that they desired to touch the hem ‘as the woman with the bloody issue did’ (Matthew 9:20), partly to show their faith and partly from a sense of unworthiness to be otherwise noticed. Barnes ties the fringe or border to the Mosaic command of Numbers 15:38 and gathers the cross-references of healing by touch (Mark 3:10, Luke 6:19, Acts 19:12). Ellicott notes the wide-spread belief was the natural result of the earlier miracle in Matthew 9:20, and that since the touch implied the faith which is the condition of receptivity, it was ‘effective.’ Clarke marvels at the ‘mighty influence’ of Christ’s grace, that even the hem of His garment wrought such wonders, and prays ‘Lord, increase our faith!’

Barnes’s extended remarks on the chapter draw together its lessons — the power of conscience in Herod, the duty of faithfulness, Christ’s care for the poor, the propriety of seeking a blessing on our food, and that ‘Jesus only can make us perfectly whole’ — applying the verse to the saving of the soul.

The shared, dominant note is that Christ’s cures here are perfect cures, and an abundance of healing virtue is in Him for all who apply, however many. The faith that touches even the hem is rewarded with complete healing, on terms so easy that He may be said to ‘heal freely’; and the bodily healing is read, by these voices, as a parable of the salvation Christ works in the soul that comes to Him by faith. Barnes gathers the parallels of healing by touch and grounds the fringe in the Mosaic command, while Clarke prays ‘Lord, increase our faith’; the witnesses agree that the faith which touches even the hem meets with a perfect and abundant cure, the bodily healing standing as a parable of the salvation Christ works in every soul that comes to Him.
